Title: 54-year-old man nabbed for exposing himself to two girls
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Johor police have arrested a 54-year-old man for allegedly exposing himself to two underage girls while seated in his car in Bandar Baru Uda here yesterday.
In the 1pm incident, a closed-circuit television recording installed at a house in the housing area
captured a black car
idling along Jalan Padi Mahsuri 10 as two girls walked past.
Seconds later, the driver called out to the girls. One of them approached the vehicle before both were seen running away. The driver then reversed and fled the scene.
Johor Baru (North) police chief Assistant Commissioner Radin Ramlan Radin Taha said the suspect was arrested at 11pm the same day at a house in Masai, following a report lodged by one of the girls' fathers.
"Initial investigations revealed that the incident involved girls aged 10 and 12.
"A 33-year-old man claimed his daughter and niece were approached by a man in a black Honda Accord. The suspect had asked for directions to a toilet before exposing himself to the victims," he said in a statement today.
The suspect will be remanded today to facilitate investigations under Section 15(a)(i) of the Sexual Offences Against Children Act 2017 for non-physical sexual acts, including indecent exposure.
If convicted, the offender faces up to 10 years' imprisonment, a fine of up to RM20,000, or both.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Maxim e-hailing operating legally nationwide
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Maxim e-hailing platform is now operating legally nationwide after receiving official approval from the relevant authorities.
Maxim Malaysia director Mohd Hazwan Musley said the company has obtained a valid licence and fully complied with the regulatory requirements stipulated by the Ministry of Transport (MOT), the Road Transport Department (JPJ), the Land Public Transport Agency (APAD) and the Commercial Vehicle Licensing Board (CVLB).
He said that on Dec 2, 2025, APAD published an official statement on Facebook listing all registered e-hailing companies operating in Peninsular Malaysia, and Maxim was also listed as having a valid permit.
He added that for its operations in Sabah and Sarawak, the company has all the required operational documents from the CVLB, and is always fully compliant with local regulatory requirements.
"Publishing the official APAD list has dispelled any doubts about Maxim's licensing status. We are operating with complete transparency and strict compliance with Malaysia's transport regulations.
"Our focus now is to continue improving service quality and safety standards, thus ensuring both drivers and passengers benefit from a trustworthy and valid e-hailing platform," he said in a statement today.
Mohd Hazwan also emphasised that all Maxim drivers are required to have a Public Service Vehicle (PSV) Licence, E-Hailing Vehicle Permit (EVP) and insurance, in addition to their vehicles having to pass the Computerised Vehicle Inspection Centre (PUSPAKOM) inspection to ensure passenger safety.
According to the statement, Maxim has also enhanced access to transportation, especially in rural areas, while providing flexible income opportunities for local drivers and supporting the local economy.
He added that the company has also implemented its corporate social responsibility (CSR) initiative in the fields of education, health and environmental sustainability.
Meanwhile, Maxim is also offering inclusive features, such as special fares for people with disabilities (PwD) and travel for women in selected areas.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Myanmar refugee's death ruled homicide: US health officials
Article:
WASHINGTON: Health officials on Wednesday ruled the death of a Rohingya refugee from Myanmar who was found dead on a street in New York state after his release from immigration detention a homicide.
On Feb 24, the body of 56-year-old Nurul Amin Shah Alam was discovered in Buffalo, in northeastern New York state, sparking a public outcry against the Trump administration's harsh immigration crackdown to expel undocumented migrants.
Local media reported at the time that US Border Patrol agents had dropped off Shah Alam – described as almost blind and unable to speak English – in frigid weather five days earlier at a restaurant.
He was found dead four miles (six kilometres) away.
After an investigation, the Erie County Medical Examiner's office determined that the cause of death was "complications of a perforated duodenal ulcer precipitated by hypothermia and dehydration," the county's Department of Health said in a statement.
The manner of death – how the cause came about – was homicide, it said.
"For death certification purposes, 'homicide' refers to death resulting from the volitional act of another, which may include negligent acts or omissions. The designation of homicide does not imply intent to cause harm or death," it said.
Shah Alam's next of kin were notified about the findings, the county health department said.
Buffalo Mayor Sean Ryan called it "deeply disturbing and a dereliction of duty" by immigration agents.
But Customs and Border Protection said immigration agents established that Shah Alam could not be deported and therefore offered him a lift to a "warm, safe location near his last known address."
"He showed no signs of distress, mobility issues or disabilities requiring special assistance," the agency said at the time.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Indonesia calls Israel death penalty law 'grave violation' of human rights
Article:
JAKARTA: Indonesia has criticised Israel's approval of a death penalty bill applicable to Palestinians in the occupied West Bank as a "grave violation of international human rights and humanitarian law."
Under the law, passed in parliament on Monday, Palestinians in the occupied West Bank convicted by military courts of carrying out deadly attacks classified as "terrorism" will face the death penalty as a default sentence.
It has been criticised by the United Nations and European Union, while the United States came out in support of "Israel's sovereign right to determine its own laws."
In a statement published on X on Wednesday, Indonesia's foreign ministry urged Israel to revoke the law and reaffirmed "its full support for the struggle of the Palestinian people to achieve independence."
Indonesia has lost three peacekeepers in recent days during fighting between Israeli troops and Hizbollah in Lebanon, part of the conflict across the Middle East sparked by US-Israeli attacks on Iran over a month ago.
"Indonesia also calls on the international community, in particular the United Nations, to take firm measures to ensure accountability and protection for the Palestinian people," Jakarta said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Results proof syllabus is effective'
Article:
SIJIL Pelajaran Malaysia (SPM) candidates have bounced back from years of Covid-19 pandemic disruption, achieving the best national average grade (GPK) since the introduction of Kurikulum Standard Sekolah Menengah (KSSM) in 2021.
They are proof of the success of the new curriculum with schoolbased assessments, being the first batch of candidates who did not sit for any major examinations till the SPM level.
Education director-general Datuk Dr Mohd Azam Ahmad said the GPK improved to 4.42 from 4.49 in 2024 and 4.86 in 2021.
For SPM, grade points are assigned to each grade, with lower numbers representing higher achievement. A lower GPK number indicates better overall results.
"SPM 2025 candidates have undergone a long and continuous teaching and learning process.
"Thanks to the unwavering dedication of teachers who continuously implemented various interventions to develop students' potential, the candidates nurtured in classrooms have emerged resilient and confident, even though SPM is the only major examination in their schooling journey," he said.
A total of 13,779 candidates earned A+, A and A- grades in all subjects last year, compared with 13,827 in 2024.
[[nid:1408710]]
However, 376 candidates achieved straight A+ results, up from 352 the year before.
Azam highlighted the achievement gap between urban and rural candidates that narrowed to just 0.4 points.
The GPK for urban candidates improved to 4.4 from 4.47 in 2024, whereas for rural candidates, it improved to 4.81 from 4.92 previously.
"This shows that targeted interventions and support programmes are helping students in rural areas achieve closer parity with their urban peers."
Candidates from special programmes and underserved communities also recorded notable achievements in SPM 2025.
Among Orang Asli students, 1,768 of the 2,128 candidates, or 83.13 per cent, qualified for the SPM certificate, an improvement from 1,527 candidates (79.04 per cent) in 2024.
"Five students earned straight As, 133 achieved at least a credit and 609 passed with minimum grades.
The SPM 2025 also marked a milestone for students of Sekolah Integriti and Henry Gurney School.
For the first time, a candidate each from these schools scored straight As, with 24 students achieving at least a credit and 63 passing with minimum grades.
"These schools face unique circumstances, and the continued progress demonstrates that focused support, skilled teachers and structured interventions can make a real difference."
Azam said the improvements reflect the resilience of the pandemic cohort, who faced disrupted schooling in their early secondary years, and the dedication of teachers who provided consistent support.
Coordination between schools, district education offices and the ministry has ensured interventions—from professional development to programmes for students at risk—were implemented effectively.
"This collaborative approach has been key in developing students' potential. The ministry is committed to ensuring no student is left behind.
"Efforts have been intensified to close the urban-rural gap through comprehensive infrastructure development, deployment of qualified teachers, equitable distribution of intervention programmes and countless other initiatives.
"These efforts are complex, but the ministry will remain steadfast in its pursuit of educational excellence and reducing gaps between urban and rural learners."
In total, 413,299 candidates registered for SPM 2025, an increase from 402,918 in 2024.
Of these, 366,435 candidates, or 94.29 per cent, qualified for the SPM 2025 certificate, compared with 355,933 candidates, or 94.01 per cent, in 2024.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Chief Justice: AI is a tool, not a replacement for human judgment
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The legal profession must guard against becoming overly dependent on artificial intelligence (AI), Chief Justice Datuk Seri Wan Ahmad Farid Wan Salleh said, cautioning that technology should not erode the human element in the practice of law.
He said while AI is increasingly embedded in legal work, there is a growing risk of allowing it to take over core thinking processes, instead of using it as a supporting tool.
"Technology makes things faster, but I dare say it can make us less human. To be human is to learn, to err, to struggle and to remember.
"When we outsource our thinking to machines, we are not just saving time — we are losing a part of ourselves.
"Efficiency is for machines. Effort is for humble mortals like us," he said in his speech at the Commonwealth Legal Education Association Conference 2026 at Universiti Malaya today.
Present were Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim, Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said, and Universiti Malaya pro-chancellor Tan Sri Datuk Zainun Ali.
Wan Ahmad Farid also emphasised the importance of discipline in thinking carefully, articulating clearly and engaging meaningfully, noting that technology cannot replicate human judgment.
"We cannot ignore technology — artificial intelligence, digital systems. These are no longer concepts of the future. They are already part of our present. But let me say this clearly — we should not fear this progress.
"Technology can assist us, but it cannot replace us. It cannot weigh compassion. It cannot exercise conscience. And it certainly cannot fully understand the human story behind every case."
He said this was why ethical reasoning must be embedded from the outset in how the law is taught and practised.
"Because ultimately, we are not just training lawyers to argue well. We are shaping individuals who carry hope and uphold justice," he added.
Earlier, Anwar opened the conference, which brings together legal practitioners, academics, policymakers, regulators and legal educators from across the Commonwealth and other jurisdictions to deliberate on contemporary developments and emerging challenges in legal education and practice.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Digital resilience a national security priority
Article:
MOST of the time, telecommunications networks operate quietly in the background. However, the moment connectivity fails, it becomes a national issue within minutes.
Digital payments stop, emergency services face disruption and public communication breaks down. What was once seen as a technical failure quickly escalates into a national concern.
This is why the conversation around digital infrastructure security must evolve. Today, protecting networks is not just about preventing data breaches — it is about ensuring continuity.
National security increasingly depends on keeping essential digital services online and maintaining public trust in systems people rely on every day.
Telecommunications networks are no longer just commercial infrastructure. They underpin modern society, carrying emergency calls, enabling banking, supporting healthcare and powering transport systems.
Governments increasingly recognise telecommunication networks as National Critical Information Infrastructure (NCII), alongside energy and water systems.
This classification reflects a simple reality: when the networks fail, the impact cascades across multiple sectors simultaneously.
The damage is rarely contained. Connectivity has therefore become a matter of national resilience, economic stability and public safety.
Beyond resilience, telecommunications infrastructure is increasingly linked to digital sovereignty — a nation's ability to maintain control over critical systems, set its own security standards and ensure that essential services remain operable under all circumstances.
In an era of geopolitical tension and cross-border cyber threats, infrastructure autonomy and operational assurance are no longer abstract concepts; they are core components of national strategy.
For decades, cybersecurity has been framed around confidentiality, integrity and availability. While all three remain important, the rise of NCII has shifted the balance.
In critical infrastructure, availability increasingly takes precedence. A secure but unavailable network still fails the nation.
Across many countries, cybersecurity strategies are shifting toward operational resilience. The focus is moving beyond compliance and breach prevention toward sustaining services under pressure.
Success is no longer defined solely by whether an attack was blocked, but by how well essential systems continue functioning during disruption and how quickly they recover.
Threats have evolved accordingly. Attackers are no longer focused only on stealing data; many now target disruption itself.
Even brief outages can halt emergency response, freeze financial systems and erode public confidence. While users may tolerate unseen security controls, they will not accept unreliable services.
Ensuring network availability cannot fall on operators alone. It requires coordinated collaboration between operators, regulators and technology partners.
Resilience must be engineered into networks from the outset through redundancy planning, continuous testing and clear recovery playbooks.
In Malaysia, closer collaboration across the ecosystem has been complemented by alignment with international assurance frameworks.
These frameworks developed by the Global System for Mobile Communication Association (GSMA) and 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP), support a transparent, structured approach to strengthening trust while maintaining operator accountability and regulatory oversight.
Strengthening cyber resilience ultimately requires sustained public–private collaboration anchored in transparency and shared standards. Technology providers have a role to play alongside operators and policymakers in advancing secure-by-design engineering and capability development.
Equally important is the development of cybersecurity talent — building a skilled workforce capable of operating, protecting and continuously improving the critical digital infrastructure that nations increasingly depend on.
Technology providers, including long-term infrastructure partners in Malaysia, have worked closely with local operators to support network evolution from 4G to 5G, emphasising resilience and security as foundational design principles.
Such long-term collaboration reflects a broader industry responsibility to embed trust and reliability into the infrastructure that underpins national progress.
Ultimately, safeguarding telecommunications networks is not optional — it is foundational to national security, economic stability and societal resilience. Nations that prioritise availability and operational readiness today will be far better prepared for tomorrow's disruptions.
Connectivity resilience has moved beyond infrastructure management. It now sits at the core of economic security and national sovereignty.
* The writer is Cybersecurity and Privacy Officer (CSPO), Huawei Malaysia
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cops: Drunk driving rooted in attitude, not just enforcement
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Alcohol consumption is not merely a matter of personal choice but carries moral responsibility and implications for public safety, police said.
Bukit Aman Traffic Enforcement and Investigation Department (TEID) director Datuk Seri Muhammed Hasbullah Ali said the issue of drunk driving must be addressed at its root through stronger values and discipline among road users.
"The main challenge is not just enforcement on the ground, but shaping awareness, responsibility and moral values among road users.
"The attitude of individuals who continue to take lightly the offence of driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs remains a key factor contributing to such cases," he said when contacted.
He said the most effective solution lies in early intervention through education and the cultivation of responsible behaviour within the community.
"As such, efforts must begin at the grassroots level through the development of strong discipline and responsible attitudes," he said.
Hasbullah added that police will continue to work closely with relevant stakeholders, including non-governmental organisations and the media, to strengthen road safety messaging.
He said such collaboration is vital in delivering effective advocacy campaigns and instilling deeper public awareness of the dangers of drink-driving.
Low public awareness and complacent attitudes towards road safety have been identified as key factors behind persistent cases involving intoxicated drivers.
His remarks follow a
fatal crash in Klang
in which motorcyclist Amirul Hafiz Omar was killed along Jalan Raya Barat after being struck by a driver believed to be under the influence of alcohol. The driver also tested positive for drugs.
Preliminary investigations found that a Honda City driven by a man in his 20s was travelling at high speed from Jalan Raya Barat towards Klang town. He is believed to have overtaken several vehicles before entering the opposite lane and colliding with a motorcycle coming from the opposite direction.
The impact threw the victim onto a Perodua Myvi. He died at the scene.
The driver, R. Saktygaanapathy, has since
been charged with murder
over the incident.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Nearly 900 cases recorded during Hari Raya price control scheme
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: A total of 896 cases were recorded during the Festive Season Maximum Price Scheme (SHMMP) for Hari Raya Aidilfitri 2026, involving compounds totaling RM99,800 and seizures valued at RM32,015.70.
Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry Enforcement director-general Datuk Azman Adam said the 15-day scheme, which ran from March 14 to 28, saw 13,194 inspections nationwide—a significant increase from the 7,258 inspections conducted last year.
"We have intensified monitoring to ensure price stability in the market. Enforcement officers were deployed to oversee producers, wholesalers, and retailers to ensure compliance throughout the SHMMP implementation," he said.
Azman noted that the most common offence was the failure to display pink price tags, accounting for 892 cases.
Pink price tags are enforced by the ministry to identify controlled-price items during festive seasons.
Only two cases involved failing to display any price tags, while another two cases were recorded for selling items above the maximum price.
He added that despite the offences, the majority of traders nationwide complied with the ceiling prices set for the 27 gazetted controlled items.
However, Azman highlighted that external factors—including the Middle East conflict affecting global oil prices, disruptions to international trade routes, and rising logistics and food import costs—have pressured the production and distribution of essential goods.
"Despite these challenges, the implementation of SHMMP has allowed the public to purchase essential goods at reasonable prices while ensuring an adequate supply," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MCMC probes multiple cases of fake news on fuel, electricity price hikes
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) is investigating eight individuals suspected of spreading false information on alleged increases in fuel and electricity prices via social media platforms.
In a statement, MCMC said five individuals are being investigated over misleading claims of a hike in petrol and diesel prices circulated on Threads, while three others are linked to false content on electricity tariff increases shared on Facebook, Threads and WhatsApp.
According to complaints received, the posts were found to be false, misleading and capable of creating public anxiety, particularly amid ongoing concerns over the cost of living.
Initial checks found that the content had been manipulated using graphic elements resembling those of established media organisations.
In the fuel price case, Buletin TV3 lodged a complaint after its visual identity was used in doctored content circulating online. The broadcaster has denied publishing the claims, describing the material as fabricated by third parties.
Similarly, in the electricity tariff case, the misleading posts were traced to edited material originating from Astro Awani.
MCMC said three of the five individuals linked to the fuel price claims have already been called in to provide statements as part of the ongoing investigation.
All cases are being investigated under Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998, which provides for a maximum fine of RM500,000, imprisonment of up to two years, or both, upon conviction.
The commission said it views seriously any misuse of digital platforms to mislead the public and warned that firm action will be taken against those found to have violated the law.
It also urged the public to exercise responsibility when using social media and to refrain from sharing unverified content that could undermine social harmony and public order.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: SP Setia launches Hari Raya sales campaign
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: SP Setia Bhd has rolled out a Hari Raya Aidilfitri sales campaign featuring buyer rewards, referral incentives and festive activities aimed at boosting homeownership and community engagement.
The campaign, running from March 16 to April 30, 2026, targets new homebuyers, existing purchasers under its "Citizen Setia" programme, and the wider public.
A key highlight is the "Rezeki Bonanza" initiative, which offers a RM4,000 reward per unit to buyers who book participating Setia properties during the campaign period and sign the sale and purchase agreement within 30 days.
Commenting on the campaign, SP Setia president and chief executive officer Datuk Zaini Yusoff said the Aidilfitri campaign underscores the group's focus on making quality homeownership more attainable.
He said this is achieved through stronger purchase incentives, timely sale and purchase agreement (SPA) conversions and added benefits for both new buyers and Citizen Setia members.
"It also reflects our long-term commitment to delivering sustainable homes and well-connected townships that create enduring value for purchasers," he said in a statement.
This festive season, the group is also expanding support beyond property ownership to the broader homeowner journey.
In partnership with Maybank, purchasers can access Maybank MyDeco financing, a flexible solution designed to ease the transition from a new property into a fully furnished home.
Maybank MyDeco financing offers existing Maybank mortgage customers up to 30 per cent additional margin of finance, or up to 120 per cent of the property value, with a repayment period of up to 10 years.
The financing covers interior design, décor, furnishing and fitting works, allowing buyers to design, furnish and personalise their new homes with greater flexibility.
Zaini said the group believes the joy of homeownership begins long before the keys are handed over, starting with a vision of a place to gather, grow and create meaningful moments.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] DoE to see new director-general after top 2 officers face court charges
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: A new Department of Environment (DoE) director-general is expected to be appointed after the department's two most senior officers are charged in court tomorrow.
Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said the matter would be left to the Chief Secretary to the Government, but he expects a new appointment once the charges are filed.
"I leave the matter to the Chief Secretary, but I expect a new director-general to be appointed after both are charged in court," he said on the sidelines of a special engagement session here today.
In January, the DoE director-general and his deputy were detained by the MACC to assist in investigations into electronic waste (e-waste) issues.
The probe focuses on alleged abuse of power and corruption linked to e-waste management, believed to have taken place over several years.
On the impending charges, Azam said the deputy public prosecutor had granted consent for both officers to be charged at the Kuala Lumpur Sessions Court tomorrow.
"I hope there will be no cancellation and that they will be charged tomorrow, if there are no complications," he said.
Last month, the MACC submitted investigation papers recommending charges against two senior DoE officials over alleged corruption linked to e-waste and plastic waste management.
The papers, involving the department's director-general and deputy director-general, were finalised and sent to the Attorney-General's Chambers (A-GC).
The case stems from long-standing issues in the management of e-waste and plastic waste, particularly in areas affected by unregulated disposal and processing activities.
It was reported that the MACC had frozen a corporate account and a personal bank account involving RM1 million in connection with the investigation.
The probe involves the DoE director-general, deputy director-general, an assistant officer and a civilian.
Sources said investigations by the MACC Investigation Division, which began on Jan 19, also led to the seizure of about RM170,000 in cash.
Investigations are now focusing on alleged abuse of power and corruption involving the failure to take enforcement action against scheduled waste management companies suspected of illegal disposal activities.
The director-general and deputy director-general were arrested on Jan 29 after giving their statements at the commission's headquarters in Putrajaya.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two senior DoE officials to be charged tomorrow
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Department of Environment (DoE) director-general and deputy director-general are expected to be charged in court tomorrow.
Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said the Attorney General's Chambers has granted approval for prosecution.
"The consent to prosecute has been obtained and charges are expected to be filed tomorrow. I do not foresee any issues," he told reporters at the sidelines of a special engagement session here today.
Azam said the case would likely be heard at the Sessions Court in Kuala Lumpur.
MORE TO COME

Title: Major earthquake in Indonesia kills one, sparks panic
Article:
MANADO, Indonesia: A major 7.4-magnitude quake struck off the coast of eastern Indonesia on Thursday, killing one person, damaging buildings and sending people running into the streets in panic.
The early-morning tremor in the Molucca Sea between the Sulawesi and Maluku island groups triggered waves up to 75 centimetres (2.5 feet) high in places and prompted a tsunami warning that was subsequently lifted.
"I didn't know what to do. I was just trying to save my family," street food vendor Siti Rohayati, 58, told AFP of the moment the quake hit during the breakfast rush in Manado in North Sulawesi province.
"All that mattered was getting my children away safely. I pushed all three of them and told them: 'Run!'"
One person was killed when a building collapsed in Manado, and another was injured, search and rescue official George Leo Mercy Randang told AFP.
The victim was "buried under the rubble", he said, adding that authorities were following up on reports of more people injured.
The US Geological Survey (USGS) said the tremor hit at a shallow depth of 35 kilometres.
The Hawaii-based Pacific Tsunami Warning Centre (PTWC) initially said hazardous tsunami waves were possible within 1,000 kilometres (621 miles) of the epicentre along the coasts of Indonesia, the Philippines and Malaysia.
Elevated waves were observed in nine locations in North Maluku, North Sulawesi and Gorontalo provinces, with the highest hitting 75 centimetres in North Minahasa, according to Indonesia's BMKG geological agency.
The PTWC lifted its warning just over two hours after the tremor, saying the tsunami threat "has now passed."
Budi Nurgianto, a 42-year-old resident of Ternate in North Maluku province, said he was inside his house when the tremor struck, sending people outside in terror.
"The quake was felt strongly. I heard it first from the walls of the house that shook," he said.
"When I went outside, there were many people outside. They were panicked. The quake was felt (for) quite long, more than a minute.
"I even saw some people leaving their house without having finished their shower."
- Aftershocks -
Dozens of aftershocks rippled through the area after the main quake, Teuku Faisal Fathani, the head of the geological agency, told journalists in the capital Jakarta.
One of them had a magnitude of 5.9, USGS data showed.
An AFP journalist in Manado, about 300 kilometres west of Ternate by sea, said the shaking woke him and others in the city of some 450,000 people.
"I immediately woke up and left my house. People (were) immediately scrambling outside. There is a school and the pupils rushed outside," he said.
The shaking persisted for "quite long" but he did not witness "significant damage", he added.
The PTWC had initially warned that tsunami waves of up to one metre were possible for parts of Indonesia, and smaller ones were possible for the Philippines, Malaysia, Japan, Taiwan, Guam and Palau.
The Japan Meteorological Agency said it expected "slight changes" in the sea level along the Pacific coast from northern Hokkaido to southern Okinawa, but has not issued any warnings.
The earthquake centres of the Philippines and Malaysia have also not issued tsunami alerts.
Indonesia and neighbouring countries experience frequent earthquakes due to their location in the Pacific "Ring of Fire" – an arc of intense seismic activity where tectonic plates collide that stretches from Japan through Southeast Asia and across the Pacific basin.
In 2004, a magnitude-9.1 quake struck the westernmost Aceh province, causing a tsunami and killing more than 170,000 people in Indonesia. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] MATF records 3,523 arrests, RM6.87bil in seizures from 2023 to 2025
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC), as lead agency of the Multi-Agency Task Force (MATF), recorded 3,523 arrests between 2023 and 2025, highlighting the country's intensified crackdown on corruption and financial crimes.
MACC chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said the figure reflected more than enforcement statistics; it demonstrates the effectiveness of a coordinated, whole-of-government approach in tackling high-profile and complex criminal networks.
"Within this period, 3,523 arrests were made, involving major operations such as Ops Samba 2.0, Ops Taufan, Ops Yellow, Ops Counter Setting and Ops Bunga.
More importantly, the success in dismantling large-scale organised syndicates through operations like Ops White, Ops Kingfruit, Ops X, Ops Sikaro, Ops Metal, Ops Karen and Ops Grip shows that MATF's approach is capable of penetrating complex and structured criminal networks across multiple sectors," he said during a special engagement session today.
Azam added that in terms of asset recovery, MACC recorded seizures amounting to RM6.87 billion in 2025 alone, alongside asset freezing, forfeiture, compounds and settlements involving substantial value.
"This sends a clear message that any proceeds obtained through wrongdoing will be traced and returned to the country," he said.
He added that as MATF's lead agency, MACC also spearheaded several high-impact operations, including Operasi 82S, Ops Tropicana, Ops Eagle and Ops Liter.
Azam said a whole-of-government approach is now essential in addressing evolving crime trends, with the success of MATF proving that integrated enforcement could not only curb criminal activities but dismantle entire syndicate ecosystems.
On illegal e-waste and plastic waste smuggling, Azam said investigations under Ops Metal revealed that Malaysia had been used as a dumping ground for illicit waste from abroad.
He said the activities involved document falsification, cargo misdeclaration and abuse of import permits, with indications of corruption at enforcement levels that allowed containers to pass through entry points without proper inspection.
"This is not merely an environmental issue. It is an economic and sovereignty issue, as it exposes the country to pollution risks while causing revenue losses and reputational damage internationally," he said.
He also highlighted cases involving diesel smuggling under Ops Karen and tyre smuggling under Ops Grip, which resulted in tax leakages amounting to hundreds of millions of ringgit.
"What is more concerning is how these syndicates were able to operate over long periods, indicating systemic weaknesses, process manipulation and possible involvement of enablers," he said.
On the issue of "corporate mafia", Azam said such groups operate discreetly through legitimate corporate structures to evade enforcement.
"They are supported by networks of professionals, including accountants, lawyers, consultants and insiders within certain agencies.
What we are facing today is no longer conventional crime, but an organised ecosystem driven by what I term as 'corporate mafia'.
They not only shield illegal activities but also manipulate corporate structures, launder illicit funds and exploit systems to ensure crimes continue undetected," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Azam declines to comment on contract extension
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki has declined to comment on matters relating to his contract extension.
When approached on the sidelines of a special engagement session at the MACC headquarters today, Azam reiterated that he would not address questions concerning the issue.
"No comment. I have already said that I will not answer on that matter," he told reporters briefly.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Economic strain could drive rise in corruption, warns Azam Baki
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Economic pressures could lead to a rise in corruption and financial crimes if governance is not strengthened, Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki has warned.
He said periods of economic strain tend to heighten financial stress among the public, creating conditions that may drive an increase in illicit activities.
"When economic difficulties occur, financial pressures tied to survival increase. At that point, corruption and organised financial crimes are likely to rise, and governance may be pushed aside if we are not careful," he said in his address at a special engagement session here today.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MMA: GP fee revision crucial step to strengthen primary care
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Medical Association (MMA) has welcomed the gazettement of new regulations revising general practitioner (GP) consultation fees, describing it as a long-overdue move to strengthen primary care.
The amendment, under P.U. (A) 150/2026 to Schedule 7 of the Private Healthcare Facilities and Services Regulations 2006, takes effect today.
MMA said GP consultation fees had remained unchanged since 2006, based on a structure dating back to 1992, and were no longer sustainable under current healthcare demands.
The association also expressed appreciation to Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad for his continued engagement, as well as Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim for announcing the revision under Budget 2026.
It acknowledged the Health Ministry, particularly its Medical Practice Division, for completing the revision process.
MMA said the revised fee structure introduces flexibility, allowing consultation charges to better reflect clinical complexity, market conditions and patient needs.
However, it said that flexibility should not be seen as a uniform increase, noting that fees would vary depending on the nature and complexity of care.
"A simple consultation is not the same as managing a complex chronic condition and should not be priced the same," it said in a statement.
The association said patients may have concerns over the revision but assured that not all consultations would see an increase.
It added that many patients currently access care through corporate panels managed by third-party administrators, where rates have remained outdated and no longer reflect current medical practice.
MMA said it would continue engaging with stakeholders to ensure consultation rates remain appropriate and sustainable.
It also welcomed the Health Ministry's new guidelines on intravenous (IV) drip services in private clinics, saying they would help ensure such treatments are delivered safely, ethically and within proper outpatient scope.
MMA president Thirunavukarasu Rajoo said the revision marks an important step forward but should not be a one-off exercise.
"If we do not build a system for periodic review, we will face the same issue again in the future," he said.
He added that strengthening primary care is essential, as it forms the foundation of the healthcare system and helps prevent higher costs in the long term.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Takeaways from Trump's speech on Iran
Article:
WASHINGTON: President Donald Trump staunchly defended his handling of the month-old US-Israeli war on Iran in a prime-time address on Wednesday, saying the US military was nearing completion of its mission while also reinforcing his threats to bomb the Islamic republic back to the Stone Age.
He delivered his 19-minute speech against a backdrop of high global oil prices and his own low approval ratings.
Here are some key takeaways:
Looking for an exit – but not quite yet
Trump, facing a war-weary American public and sliding poll numbers, said the US had destroyed Iran's navy and air force, crippled its ballistic missile and nuclear programme and would continue to hit them "extremely hard" over the next two to three weeks.
But beyond that, even while saying the US military was on track to complete its objectives "very fast", he stopped short of offering a firm timeline for an end to hostilities.
And he suggested the war could escalate if Iranian leaders did not capitulate to US terms during negotiations, with strikes on Iran's energy and oil infrastructure possible.
Trump's use of his speech to reiterate threats and send mixed messages may do little to calm jittery financial markets and ease the concerns of an American public that has shown little support for the country's biggest military operation since the 2003 invasion of Iraq.
The often conflicting signals that Trump has issued throughout the course of the conflict have only added to confusion, with the president one moment calling for a diplomatic settlement and in the next threatening to rain further destruction on Iran amid a continuing US military build-up in the region.
The Strait of Hormuz
Trump's comments on Wednesday were not clear about whether US military operations could end even before Iran reopened the Strait of Hormuz, the vital waterway on which it has a chokehold that has created the worst global energy shock in history.
He instead repeated his calls for countries that rely on Gulf oil to "take the lead" and assume the burden of reopening the waterway, not the US, which he said does not need energy supplies from the region.
Western allies, however, have resisted joining a war that he and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu started without consulting them.
In his speech, however, Trump stopped short of saying, as he has in recent media interviews, that he is considering withdrawing from NATO over what he sees as its failure to support the US in the Iran conflict.
The risk, analysts say, is that Iran would essentially be left with significant leverage over the strait, the passageway for a fifth of the world's oil and natural gas shipments.
Washington's Gulf allies may also resent a hasty US exit, given that they could be left with a wounded, hostile neighbour.
Mission accomplished?
Trump touted the US military's successes in the conflict but questions remain about whether he has truly achieved the main goal he laid out at the start of the war: closing off Iran's path to a nuclear weapon.
More than a month later, Iran still has a stockpile of highly enriched uranium that could be processed to bomb grade, but it is believed to be mostly buried underground by US-Israeli bombing in June.
Trump, in a sudden reversal from his demands that Iran turn over the enriched uranium, told Reuters earlier on Wednesday that he no longer cared about the material because it was "so far underground" and US satellites could keep an eye on the area. Iran has always denied seeking a nuclear bomb.
While threatening new air strikes if Iran tries to move the stockpile, he made no mention of sending special forces on a risky mission to seize it, which US officials have said is among the options under consideration. However, any deployment of ground troops would likely anger most Americans.
Despite Trump's claims of having destroyed Iran's conventional military capabilities, it has demonstrated that its remaining missiles and drones can still be used to target Israel as well as US Gulf allies and American military installations housed on their land.
And Trump's earlier calls for the overthrow of Iran's theocratic rulers have gone unfulfilled. US-Israeli air strikes killed many of the top leaders, including supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, but they have been replaced by even more hard-line successors, including Khamenei's son. US intelligence has deemed the Iranian government largely intact.
Domestic politics
Trump's speech, his first prime-time address since the war started on February 28, was originally seen as being aimed at easing Americans' concerns about the interventionist tendencies of a president who campaigned for his second term on a promise to keep the US out of "stupid" military interventions.
But Trump, whose advisers have pressed him to show the public that he considers kitchen-table issues a priority, gave only a nod to Americans' anxieties and appeared to dismiss their economic pain as temporary and sure to ease once the war is over.
"Many Americans have been concerned to see the recent rise in gasoline prices here at home," he said. "This short-term increase has been entirely the result of the Iranian regime launching deranged terror attacks against commercial oil tankers of neighbouring countries that have nothing to do with the conflict."
While Trump's MAGA movement has mostly stood with him, his grip on his political base could weaken if the economic impact, including high gas prices, persists, with his Republican Party scrambling to keep control of Congress in November's midterm elections.
Trump's overall approval rating has fallen to 36 per cent, the lowest since his return to the White House, a Reuters/Ipsos poll completed on Monday found.
After his TV appearance, stocks fell, the dollar strengthened and oil rose as Trump stopped short of providing a clear outline for when the war would end.
The market reaction reflects a basic problem with Trump's dissonant messaging: he wants to reassure Americans that the war will be over soon, while at the same time threatening Iran with new attacks and suggesting he may leave without opening the Strait of Hormuz.
Flat performance?
Wednesday's address offered Trump precious prime-time viewership and a chance to reset with voters. He made a dramatic entrance, walking through double doors in the White House residence to approach the podium.
But for the next 19 minutes, he spoke in a mostly subdued tone in a dimly lit room, sticking to well-worn talking points instead of clarifying his reasons for taking the US to war.
It was a far cry from the usual public appearances of the former reality TV star who was in front of probably his biggest audience since February's State of the Union address.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: What's real anymore? AI warps truth of Middle East war
Article:
WASHINGTON, UNITED STATES: "Is Netanyahu real or AI?" an internet headline blared, pointing to a video that supposedly showed the Israeli prime minister with six fingers.But the clip was real.
Speculation spiraled online that Netanyahu might be dead or wounded in an Iranian strike and that Israel was covering it up with a double generated by artificial intelligence.
"Last time I checked, humans usually don't have 6 fingers... AI does," said one post on X, garnering nearly five million views. "Is Netanyahu no more?"
Digital forensics researchers were quick to explain the "extra" finger: a trick of light that made part of his palm resemble an additional digit.
But that message was largely drowned out in the online uproar. It also mattered little that advanced AI visual generators – now capable of churning out uncannily real-looking deepfakes within seconds – have largely erased the once-telltale glitch of extra fingers.
So how do you prove what's real is real when the line between reality and fabrication has blurred so much in the fog of the Middle East war?
A few days later, Netanyahu posted another video – a proof-of-life clip from a coffee shop.
He held both hands up as if to challenge skeptics to count his fingers.
But instead of quelling the speculation, the video fueled a new wave of unfounded theories.
"More AI," said one viral Threads post, questioning why his cup remained full after a large sip.
Suspicion reigned even after Netanyahu posted a third video, this one with the US ambassador to Israel, Mike Huckabee.
Some online sleuths zoomed in on Netanyahu's ears, claiming their shape and size did not match older images.
'Same footing as hearsay'
AFP's global network has produced more than 500 debunks of false information in multiple languages since the conflict began – a rate never before seen in such a crisis. Between a quarter and a fifth of them used AI.
The Russian invasion of Ukraine, the Israel-Gaza war and the conflict between India and Pakistan all triggered waves of AI-generated content.
What sets the Middle East war apart is the sheer volume – and realism – of AI images produced by advanced tools that are cheap and capable of eliminating many of the old signs of manipulation, researchers say.
Tech platforms are now saturated with what is widely dubbed "AI slop."
The result is a deepening crisis of trust as hyper-realistic AI fabrications compete for attention with – and often drown out – authentic images and videos.
"I think at this time we all need to start treating photos, video and audio on the same footing as hearsay," Thomas Nowotny, who leads an AI research group at the University of Sussex in the UK, told AFP.
The issue for Constance de Saint Laurent, a professor at Ireland's Maynooth University, "is not so much that people believe" disinformation, it is "that they see real news and they don't trust it anymore."
'Harmful content'
The volume of fakes has largely outpaced the verification capacity of professional fact-checkers.
The work often feels like a game of whack-a-mole. Debunked claims routinely resurface across platforms awash with fakes, a pattern some researchers call "zombie" misinformation.
Algorithms amplify content based on engagement – and engagement is often driven by sensationalism, outrage and misinformation.
Social media platforms "act as editors through what they decide to show to their users, primarily through their feed. And very often, that includes harmful content and misinformation," said Saint Laurent.
Financial incentives further accelerate the problem. Some platforms, including X, allow creators to earn revenue based on engagement, encouraging influencers to push misleading or entirely fabricated content for clicks.
[[nid:1408653]]
According to the London-based Institute for Strategic Dialogue (ISD), a network of X accounts posting AI content about the Middle East war has amassed more than one billion views since the conflict began.
In another viral example, an X account posted an AI video appearing to show Dubai's Burj Khalifa skyscraper collapsing in a cloud of dust.
"10 million views and no Community Note. We cooked ya'll," information warfare analyst Tal Hagin wrote on X 20 hours after it was posted.
By the time a Community Note – a crowd-sourced verification system, whose effectiveness has been repeatedly questioned by researchers – was appended to the post a few hours later, the video had more than 12 million views.
Synthetic content has continued to proliferate on X even after the Elon Musk-owned platform announced that it would penalize creators – suspending them from its revenue-sharing program for 90 days – if they post AI war videos without a label.
'Legofication'
Meme-driven AI content that trivializes conflict as it spreads misinformation is increasingly crowding out reality on digital platforms, in what ISD researchers call the "Legofication" of war propaganda.
A spoof Iranian AI "Lego Movie" went viral in the first week of the war, accusing US President Donald Trump of attacking Tehran to distract from his role in the Jeffrey Epstein scandal.
Lifelike meme videos have also been used to depict fictional Iranian military victories and even the strategic Strait of Hormuz reimagined as a cartoonish toll booth.
Trump has himself warned that AI has become a "disinformation weapon that Iran uses quite well."
"Buildings and Ships that are shown to be on fire are not - It's FAKE NEWS, generated by AI," he wrote on Truth Social.
Yet the US president has hugely embraced the technology, sharing AI-generated images and videos to portray himself as a king and Superman, while casting opponents as criminals or laughingstocks.
He has also used AI memes to fuel conspiracy theories and false narratives.
Meanwhile, coordinated information operations linked to Russia are exploiting the online chaos, impersonating trusted media outlets such as the BBC to spread falsehoods, according to the ISD.
Inciting violence
"We believe tech platforms are not currently doing enough to help users identify whether content is AI-generated or authentic," Meta's Oversight Board, the body created by Facebook to review content moderation decisions, said last month.
"Fake content can be harmful by inciting more violence and fueling further conflict," it added.
AFP works in 26 languages with Facebook's fact-checking program, including in Asia, Latin America and the European Union.
Meta ended its third-party fact-checking program in the US last year, with chief executive Mark Zuckerberg saying it had led to "too much censorship" – a claim strongly rejected by proponents of the program.
Instead, Zuckerberg said Meta's platforms, Facebook and Instagram, would use the "Community Notes" model – a move critics argue could further weaken safeguards against misinformation.
Meta's Oversight Board warned that expanding the model outside the United States could pose "significant human rights risks and contribute to tangible harms" to people living under repression or conflict.
'Liar's dividend'
AI detection tools were meant to cut through the fog of the information war. Instead, they are sometimes making it denser.
In the Netanyahu case, conspiracy theorists pointed to an AI detection tool that falsely labeled his coffee shop video as "96.9 percent AI-generated." Other tools reached the opposite conclusion.
[[nid:1408652]]
The problem extends beyond videos. Social media is rife with fabricated satellite imagery, heatmaps and other pseudo forensic visuals used to cast doubt on genuine evidence from the war, researchers say.
"The rise of AI deepfakes and the dismissal of real footage are two sides of the same coin," said Sofia Rubinson, of misinformation watchdog NewsGuard.
"When everything could be fake, it becomes easy to believe that anything is."
Social media users have falsely accused leading media organizations such as the New York Times of publishing AI-generated conflict images, including one that showed a large crowd in Tehran celebrating the new Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei.
Those who benefit from misinformation can easily exploit this – a phenomenon researchers call the "liar's dividend," where genuine but unflattering information is waved away as AI-generated.
"Don't let AI technology undermine your willingness to trust anything you see and hear," said Hannah Covington, senior director of education content at the nonprofit News Literacy Project.
"That's what bad actors want: for people to think that everything can be faked, so they can't trust anything," Covington told AFP.
Signs of that shift are already visible, as fake images of real incidents further pollute the information landscape.
After a deadly strike on an elementary school in the city of Minab on February 28, an official Iranian account on X posted a photograph showing a child's backpack smeared with blood and dust.
AFP found the image was very likely AI-generated. But few online seemed troubled that a fabricated image had been used to depict the deaths of real schoolchildren.
"Likely AI edited, but the meaning is real," one Reddit user wrote.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MACC completes probe into White & Case over 1MDB-linked funds, recommends charges
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has completed its investigation into international law firm White & Case over its alleged links to misappropriated 1Malaysia Development Bhd (1MDB) funds, with a recommendation for charges to be filed against several individuals.
The probe, conducted domestically, uncovered elements of offences. However, investigations are ongoing to obtain additional evidence from abroad before any final enforcement action is taken.
MACC chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said the investigation covered, among others, the preparation of documents suspected to contain false information and particulars, as well as misleading payment instructions that allegedly led to the disbursement of funds linked to 1MDB to unauthorised parties.
He said investigations also examined payments made to the firm believed to involve funds directly linked to the misappropriation of 1MDB assets.
"The investigation was conducted under relevant legal provisions, including the MACC Act 2009 and the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act 2001 (AMLA)," he said when contacted.
Azam said efforts are ongoing to strengthen the case, including obtaining key information and documents through formal legal channels such as the Attorney General's Chambers (AGC), as well as via Mutual Legal Assistance (MLA) mechanisms with authorities in the United Kingdom.
He added that MACC is working closely with international agencies to secure evidence and documentation, describing such cooperation as a critical component of coordinated cross-border enforcement.
"The commission will also share its findings with relevant authorities to support further enforcement actions within their respective jurisdictions," he said.
The investigation follows earlier disclosures that MACC had intensified its probe into an international law firm linked to financial transactions involving 1MDB funds.
The firm has been tied to the alleged misappropriation of
US$1 billion in 1MDB funds,
including the diversion of around US$700 million to an offshore entity beyond 1MDB's control.
In December last year, MACC said it was probing the firm over suspected involvement in financial transactions linked to proceeds from unlawful activities involving 1MDB funds.
Sources also indicated that additional information had been obtained involving a former 1MDB executive director of business development believed to be linked to the misappropriated funds.
Azam had previously said that MACC could no longer act alone and must collaborate with international counterparts to strengthen investigations and asset recovery efforts.
"The task of an enforcement agency like ours is crucial in detecting and recovering assets linked to 1MDB," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UMW Toyota unveils new battery EV line-up, strengthens push toward low-carbon mobility
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: UMW Toyota Motor (UMWT) has unveiled its latest battery electric vehicle (BEV) lineup, marking a major step in supporting Malaysia's transition towards low-carbon mobility, in line with the National Energy Transition Roadmap.
The launch, officiated by Investment, Trade and Industry (Miti) Deputy Minister Sim Tze Tzin, underscores the broader economic and industrial importance of electrification beyond just environmental goals.
Sim said the shift towards electric mobility would reduce reliance on fossil fuels and create better jobs, strengthen innovation and position Malaysia as a competitive player in evolving the global automotive landscape.
"Based on a total industrial volume of 820,752 units in 2025, electric vehicles now account for around 5.5 per cent of new vehicle registration.
"This is why I am excited to witness Toyota's new BEV to add to the total industry volume, reflecting how Malaysia is moving beyond early adoptions toward a more sustainable phase of growth in electrified mobility," he said.
At the event, Toyota introduced three all-new electrified models, such as the Toyota bZ4X, Hilux BEV, and Urban Cruiser BEV, each representing a different segment of mobility, spanning passenger, urban, and commercial applications. This strategy highlights a diverse range of powertrain solutions designed to cater to varying consumer needs and infrastructure readiness, rather than a one-size-fits-all transition.
Sim also stressed the importance of strengthening Malaysia's EV ecosystem, including local vendor development, talent building and charging infrastructure, which has already surpassed 5,000 public charging points nationwide.
UMWT president Datuk K Ravindran said Malaysia remains at an early stage of electrification, noting that BEVs and hybrid vehicles accounted for only a small share of total vehicle sales, additionally stressing the importance of a balanced and inclusive transition.
"This is why Toyota adopts a multi-pathway strategy which includes hybrid, plug-in hybrid, battery EV and fuel cell." We offer a range of technology because the journey to carbon neutrality is not a one-size-fits-all principle, and we aim to deliver vehicles built on quality, durability and reliability," he said.
The launch reflects growing momentum in Malaysia's automotive sector towards cleaner mobility, as both public and private stakeholders accelerate efforts to reduce carbon emissions and promote sustainable transportation alternatives.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police raise alarm as drink-driving continues to turn deadly
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police recorded 27 fatal accidents involving intoxicated drivers in 2024, compared with 31 cases the previous year, underscoring persistent concerns over drink-driving despite relatively low overall figures.
Bukit Aman Traffic Enforcement and Investigation Department (TEID) director Datuk Seri Muhammed Hasbullah Ali said each case carries severe consequences, particularly the loss of life, and must not be taken lightly.
"In reality, these cases stem from avoidable behaviour, and TEID views the matter with utmost seriousness and commitment," he told New Straits Times.
In response, police are intensifying enforcement operations, including roadblocks and high-impact integrated operations, particularly during high-risk periods such as late nights and weekends.
Hasbullah said the increased deployment is aimed not only at detecting offenders but also at creating a strong deterrent effect among road users.
"Such operations are not only effective in directly identifying offenders but also serve as a psychological deterrent.
"The consistent presence of enforcement personnel at high-risk locations and times helps improve compliance with the law," he said.
On challenges faced by the department, he said there were no major issues from a legal or investigative standpoint, citing strong cooperation with the Attorney General's Chambers.
"The strong support from prosecutors ensures investigations and prosecutions are carried out smoothly and effectively.
"The main challenge lies not in enforcement, but in cultivating awareness, responsibility and moral values among road users," he added.
His remarks come amid continued fatal incidents involving suspected drink-driving.
In Klang, motorcyclist
Amirul Hafiz Omar was killed
along Jalan Raya Barat after being struck by a driver on March 29 believed to be under the influence of alcohol. The driver also tested positive for drugs.
Preliminary investigations found that a Honda City driven by a man in his 20s was travelling at high speed from Jalan Raya Barat towards Klang town. He is believed to have overtaken several vehicles before entering the opposite lane and colliding with a motorcycle coming from the opposite direction.
The impact threw the victim onto a Perodua Myvi. He died at the scene.
The driver, R. Saktygaanapathy, has since been
charged with murder
over the incident.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MAG extends profit streak as revenue climbs to RM14.5bil in 2025
Article:
The parent company of Malaysia Airlines Bhd reported a six per cent year-on-year increase in total revenue to RM14.5 billion for the financial year ended 2025 compared with the previous year.
Net income after tax (NIAT) rose to RM137 million more than doubling from RM54 million recorded in 2024 reflecting improved operational efficiency and stronger load factors across its network.
MAG president and group chief executive officer Captain Nasaruddin A. Bakar said the group entered 2025 with a clear priority to execute its long-term business plan (LTBP) 2.0 with discipline which translated into more stable operations, dependable schedules and becoming a responsive organisation.
"Following our successful financial restructuring, this discipline has not only stabilised the business, but more importantly, laid the groundwork for how we navigate both uncertainty and opportunity going forward," he said at MAG 2025 annual performance media briefing.
For 2025, MAG recorded earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortisation (EBITDA) of RM1.6 billion from RM788 million in 2024 supported by stronger Ringgit, favourable fuel prices, and capacity expansion across key markets.
The group's available seat kilometres (ASK) rose by 16 per cent while total passengers carried increased by 12 per cent to 18.6 million with a load factor of 81 per cent, which reflects strong demand across international and domestic segments.
However, MAG ended 2025 with a lower cash balance of RM1.53 billion compared to RM3 billion in 2024.
The group's chief financial officer Boo Hui Yee said the lower cash balance is primarily due to the RM2.5 billion paid for major engine overhaul cost for its Boeing B737 aircraft and other operational cost expenses.
She added that the cash balance from the RM3.6 billion injected into the company in 2021 remained at RM1.77 billion as of Dec 31, 2025.
The RM3.6 billion was pumped into the group by its sole shareholder, Khazanah Nasional Bhd following MAG's financial restructuring.
"Today, we have not drawn down everything (from the capital given by Khazanah). We have been managing our cash flow very prudently, and even though we have the ability to drawdown, we normally will actually drive operating cash flow to actually fund operations," Boo said.
As at end-2025, MAG received 24 new aircraft comprising of B737s and Airbus A330neo (new engine option).
Nasaruddin said the group is expecting another 10 new aircraft in 2026 without any delays in new aircraft deliveries.
"So far, it is still maintaining as per what has been mentioned by the manufacturers," he said, adding that MAG's widebody aircraft replacement program is still on track and the group would be making an order announcement this year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Anwar warns of 'law of the jungle' undermining global order [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has warned that the rule of law is increasingly being undermined in international affairs, with justice often overshadowed by power and selective enforcement.
He said the rule of law must remain universally accessible and enforceable, stressing that its integrity is central to a just and orderly international system.
However, he said global realities reflect a "law of the jungle", where powerful actors disregard established norms in pursuit of their own interests.
"There is deep irony here, an insidious form of hypocrisy.
"I would go even further — a pathological perversion of values, where some of the strongest advocates of the rules-based international order not only fail to observe it but manifest utter contempt for it," he said during his keynote address at the Commonwealth Legal Education Association Conference 2026 at Universiti Malaya today.
Also present were Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Minister Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said, Chief Justice Datuk Wan Ahmad Farid Wan Salleh and Universiti Malaya Pro-Chancellor Tan Sri Datuk Zainun Ali.
Anwar said that the rule of law in international relations must not be invoked selectively, warning that it cannot be used to shield one party from accountability while denying another its inherent right to self-defence.
"Its credibility depends on consistency. We should neither mince words, divert our gaze nor close our minds, as this is the reality that future lawyers will be stepping into," he said.
Meanwhile, Anwar said the legal profession must now grapple with emerging challenges, including the rise of artificial intelligence (AI) tools such as large language and reasoning models.
He said while it is important to look ahead to the future of the profession and legal education, deeper questions must not be sidelined.
"Given that we are on the cusp of a sea change, this conference must allow for meaningful exchange on how the law must evolve," he added.
Anwar's remarks come amid heightened geopolitical tensions in recent months, particularly following the escalation of conflict in the Middle East on Feb 28 after large-scale military strikes by the United States and Israel on multiple sites in Iran.
The ongoing developments have since triggered a broader regional crisis, raising concerns over global energy security and shipping routes.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sime Darby Property award to IJM a timely boost for data centre progress amid market volatility
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Sime Darby Property Bhd's contract award to IJM Corp Bhd comes at an opportune time and is expected to reassure investors that construction of the two data centre facilities in Elmina Business Park is progressing as planned.
RHB Research noted that the timing is particularly significant given the current volatility in the equity market caused by the Middle East conflict.
Yesterday, IJM's unit, IJM Construction Sdn Bhd, won a RM658 million contract from SD Property to carry out the main building works for a hyperscale data centre at Elmina Business Park.
"Investors should expect the leasing income to kick in from late 2026 onwards. Data Centre 1 and Data Centre 2 have lease values of RM2 billion and RM5.6 billion for the initial 20 years.
"We expect Sime Darby Property to finetune its stake in Data Centre 2 – given the size and the limit of its balance sheet – in order to balance its funding commitment to undertake other industrial property investment developments," it said in a note.
RHB Research noted that the first income contribution from Data Centre 1 is expected to boost Sime Darby Property's full-year earnings for the financial year 2027.
The firm added that, taking into account interest costs from the underlying borrowings for Data Centre 1, it estimates the net income from the project to be around RM40 million to RM50 million per annum in the initial years.
It added that, assuming the Middle East conflict is resolved in the near term, Sime Darby Property is expected to proceed with listing its industrial investment properties through a real estate investment trust next year, as planned.
"This would be a major rerating catalyst for the stock," RHB Research said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Health Ministry shortens wait for medical graduate placements to two months
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Health Ministry (MOH) has reduced the waiting period for medical graduate placements to two months from an average of 10 months.
The move is part of five initiatives to counter Singapore's increasingly aggressive efforts to attract Malaysian healthcare workers, including doctors.
MOH said the improvement was achieved through measures to streamline the placement process.
"MOH remains committed to ensuring that medical graduates are placed within a reasonable timeframe so they are not left without job opportunities," it said.
Among the initiatives is the introduction of a conditional offer under the Student Training Scheme (STB), allowing final-year medical students to apply early for UD9 contract medical officer posts through the Public Service Commission or their universities before graduation, helping to expedite placements.
The ministry acknowledged that some graduates still require time before full registration. An MBSB Research study found that 529 out of 5,000 respondents, or 10.5 per cent, rejected job offers in Jan 2026.
KKM said the situation reflects a growing talent shortage, particularly as Singapore steps up efforts to recruit Malaysian medical specialists.
As another measure, the ministry is offering temporary contracts of up to two years for fully registered medical officers after compulsory service, in line with provisions under the Medical Act 1971.
"The duration of the bond between the officer and the sponsoring agency is subject to the terms of the contract agreement between the parties," it said.
To improve placements, MOH is working with the Malaysian Medical Council to expedite the issuance of practising certificates, while e-placement for UD9 contract medical officers will be conducted twice a month to ensure a steady intake of graduates.
MOH said that intakes are conducted several times a year in line with academic calendars, and not only at the end of the year.
It added that about 5,000 graduates require training based on the capacity of 48 training hospitals nationwide, with placements filled progressively rather than all at once.
MOH said that based on its records since 2016, intake for housemanship training has remained consistent at between 3,000 and 6,000 medical graduates annually.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RHB sees margin upside in IJM Selangor data centre win
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: RHB Research expects IJM Corp Bhd's data centre construction contract in Selangor to generate a profit-before-tax margin of between five and eight per cent.
The research house said that, with this latest win, IJM has secured data centre projects amounting to RM5 billion on an effective share basis.
"With this job in Elmina Business Park pertaining to core and shell works for a data centre, we do not rule out it also scoring a package related to mechanical and electrical (M&E) works for the same data centre, which has yet to be awarded and could be worth RM500 million to RM700 million, in our view.
"This is because IJM secured the M&E portion for the earlier data centre (known as Package 1) in Elmina Business Park, which was awarded in Oct 2025 and was worth RM874 million, after securing the core and shell works worth RM1.3 billion for the same package," it said.
RHB Research estimates that this latest contract lifts IJM Corp's outstanding order book to RM8.2 billion.
"This is on top of its RM6.5 billion overseas orderbook from Singapore and the UK. According to our analysis, data centre jobs now make up around 45 per cent to 50 per cent of its domestic outstanding orderbook," it said.
The firm said it has made no changes to its earnings forecasts, as the latest contract win falls within its financial year 2027 job replenishment assumption of RM5 billion.
RHB Research added that IJM Corp's potential upcoming wins could come from additional data centre projects in the Klang Valley or Johor, as well as from the civil servant housing project in Indonesia's new capital, Nusantara.
"A catalyst for the stock would be if IJM Corp becomes involved in infrastructure jobs, namely the RM5 billion to RM6 billion Penang Mutiara Line Light Rail Transit cross-sea link," it said.
The firm maintained its "Buy" rating on IJM Corp, with an unchanged target price of RM3.15.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MARKET PULSE AM APRIL 2, 2026 [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia rose on Thursday, following Wall Street gains after US president Donald Trump hinted that the Middle East conflict could be nearing an end.
The FBM KLCI is expected to trade between 1,705 and 1,720 today.
An analyst said the key index may extend its rally for a third day, driven by hopes of easing tensions in Iran.
However, market sentiment remains fragile as investors weigh geopolitical risks, high energy prices, and disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz.
In the cryptocurrency market, Bitcoin tumbled to RM274,932, while Ethereum was flat at RM8,584.
That's it for Market Pulse.
